items.q: What is the difference between ozone depletion and climate change?
items.correct: Ozone depletion lets through more UV radiation; climate change is global warming from greenhouse gases.
items.misconception: They are the same environmental problem.
items.random_wrong: Ozone depletion cools the planet down.
items.why: Students merge the two distinct issues, confusing UV protection with greenhouse warming.

items.q: Is the greenhouse effect entirely harmful?
items.correct: No; the natural greenhouse effect keeps Earth warm enough for life; it is the enhanced effect that causes problems.
items.misconception: Yes; all greenhouse warming is dangerous and unnatural.
items.random_wrong: The greenhouse effect only happens inside greenhouses.
items.why: Learners treat all greenhouse warming as bad, missing that the natural effect is essential.

items.q: Does carbon dioxide cause acid rain?
items.correct: No; acid rain is mainly caused by sulfur dioxide and nitrogen oxides, not carbon dioxide.
items.misconception: Yes; carbon dioxide is the main cause of acid rain.
items.random_wrong: Acid rain is caused by oxygen in the air.
items.why: Students wrongly link all pollution problems to carbon dioxide, the most familiar pollutant.

items.q: The most abundant gas in the Earth's atmosphere is:
items.why: It's Nitrogen. “Oxygen” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: The ozone layer in the stratosphere is important because it absorbs:
items.why: Correct answer: Ultraviolet radiation. “All infrared radiation” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Acid rain is mainly caused by emissions of:
items.why: It's Sulfur dioxide and nitrogen oxides. “Carbon dioxide only” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: The enhanced greenhouse effect is driven by increased:
items.misconception: The size of the ozone layer
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Human greenhouse gas emissions. “The size of the ozone layer” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Approximately what percentage of the Earth's atmosphere is oxygen?
items.why: The real figure is 21 %. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
